warnings.title: Nested Sensitive inside a Sensitive container is unspecified and will raise at test time
warnings.location: Task 19 property test body (~line 1869-1906), redaction.py walk_model_schema
warnings.issue: The rev-4 invariant compares value_provider(raw_args) vs value_provider(redacted_args) at each Sensitive node. For the current promoted set (options: Annotated[dict[str, Any], Sensitive()], content: Annotated[str, Sensitive()]) there is no inner _SensitiveMarker inside a Sensitive container, so no problem. But if a future promotion writes Annotated[dict[str, InnerModel], Sensitive()] where InnerModel carries its own Sensitive-annotated fields, walk_model_schema will yield a marker at the outer container path AND at the inner nested-model paths. The container summarizer replaces the whole container with a string. When the property test then calls value_provider(redacted_args) for the deeper path, it will attempt to iterate a string (the summarizer output) as if it were a container of (key, value) pairs. The test has no try/except (by design — defensive except is forbidden), so this propagates as a TypeError. The test body does not specify whether Sensitive composition is recursive (outer marker short-circuits inner markers) or whether it is the implementer's responsibility to avoid double-marking. For Phase 2's current manifest scope this is latent, not live. But the first future promotion that accidentally double-marks will produce a confusing TypeError in the property test rather than a clear contract-violation message.
warnings.resolution: Two options: (a) add a walk_model_schema design note that Sensitive markers short-circuit descent (once a node is marked Sensitive, do not descend further into its type); the adequacy guard and walker both consume this iterator, so the short-circuit semantics are enforced uniformly. (b) Add an explicit constraint to ToolRedaction construction that rejects any argument_model where walk_model_schema detects a Sensitive marker inside a container field that is itself Sensitive-marked. Option (a) is simpler and aligns with the 'summarizer replaces the whole container' design intent. The plan should specify the short-circuit semantics before Phase 2 ships so future promotions are not left to discover the behaviour at test time.

warnings.title: Hypothesis strategy resolution may fail silently for some promoted tool shapes
warnings.issue: st.from_type(model) for Pydantic 2.x with model_config = ConfigDict(extra='forbid') and Annotated[dict[str, Any], Sensitive()] fields may raise hypothesis.errors.ResolutionFailed during collection for some tool argument models. When collection fails, the parametrize entry is reported as an error (or in some pytest configurations, silently skipped) rather than a test failure — the property test for that tool effectively ceases to exist in CI. The test body has no fallback strategy and no assume() call. The plan does not add a CI-level verification that all parametrize entries collect successfully. For the two currently promoted tools (set_source, which has dict[str, Any], and the Wave 2-4 blob/pipeline tools) the risk varies by field type: Hypothesis handles st.from_type for most Pydantic types but dict[str, Any] with no constraint generates arbitrary key-value pairs that may hit extra='forbid' at validate time. The parametrize-then-given pattern means a collection failure on one tool does not affect others, but the failure is invisible unless CI explicitly checks for collection errors.
warnings.resolution: Add a Task 19 pre-commit step: run pytest tests/unit/web/composer/test_redaction_completeness_property.py --collect-only and verify every parametrize entry is collected (no errors, no warnings about unresolvable strategies). Additionally, add a note to the property test's implementation guidance: if st.from_type(model) fails to resolve for any tool, use an explicit composite strategy (st.builds(Model, field_name=specific_strategy)) and document the fallback in the test. The plan should not allow the property test to silently degrade to fewer-than-all-tools coverage.

warnings.title: Done-When 22 items are unstructured; scanning is error-prone for an agentic worker
warnings.issue: The Done-When list grew from 15 to 22 items across rev-3 and rev-4 fixups. Each item is a genuine and non-redundant Phase 2 criterion. However the list is a flat sequence with no grouping. An agentic worker executing Task 19 (final gate) must scan all 22 items sequentially; the items mix concerns (walker contract, manifest population, ARG_ERROR routing, audit integrity, CI infrastructure) in the order they were added, not by subsystem. This increases the probability of a missed item at completion-time review, particularly for items that rely on grep output (the MissingRequiredPaths sweep, the _TOOL_REQUIRED_PATHS sweep) which are interspersed with test-pass items.
warnings.resolution: Group the 22 items into five named subsections within the Done-When list: (1) Walker hardening (iterator contract, optional-scalar arm, guard-parity, completeness floor-check), (2) Manifest population and adequacy (registry equality, extra=forbid, per-entry shape, hash snapshot, runtime smoke), (3) Sensitive[T] promotion wave (ARG_ERROR routing, TOOL_REQUIRED_PATHS removal grep, MissingRequiredPaths sweep), (4) Audit integrity and telemetry (Hypothesis property test with six-item checklist, canary integration scenario, summarizer SLO, telemetry counters fire), (5) Gate and governance (label-gate direction tests, compose-loop unknown-tool routing, Phase 3 call-order pin, no CODEOWNERS). This is presentational and non-blocking but has genuine execution-risk value for the agentic worker.

minor.title: _is_container_descent_path uses path-string inspection rather than field_type introspection
minor.issue: The helper _is_container_descent_path(path) checks whether the path string contains '[*]' or '{*}'. This is a contract dependency on walk_model_schema's path-notation convention rather than on get_origin(node.field_type). The plan correctly notes in the implementation commentary that this 'keeps the test decoupled from Pydantic introspection internals.' That decoupling is the right call: Pydantic internals are not stable across patch releases, and the path notation is pinned by test_walk_model_schema.py's assertion set. The coupling is explicit (both ends have test coverage) not implicit. This is a stylistic minor: the helper's docstring should cite the path-notation contract (which test_walk_model_schema.py establishes) so a future reader understands why path-string inspection is preferred over get_origin().

minor.title: @given decorating an inner function then calling it is unidiomatic
minor.location: Task 19 property test, lines ~1850-1920: @given(st.from_type(model)) def check(payload): ... check()
minor.issue: The pattern defines a @given-decorated inner function and calls it in the outer parametrize body. This is valid Hypothesis (the @given decorator wraps the function into a Hypothesis test callable; calling it runs the test). However, Hypothesis shrinking and failure attribution are designed for module-level @given test functions. When a failure occurs, the shrunk counterexample is reported against the inner function name ('check'), not the parametrize entry. This makes CI failure messages less clear ('check failed for payload=...' rather than 'test_redaction_replaces_every_sensitive_value[set_source] failed'). The pattern is used elsewhere in property-test literature as a workaround for pytest-hypothesis's lack of native parametrize+given support, so it is not a bug. It is worth noting in the implementation guidance: when a failure occurs, the parametrize entry name appears in the outer test's nodeid; the Hypothesis counterexample appears in the inner shrunk report. The developer must read both.

minor.title: Appendix chain is sustainable for future revisions; no structural concern
minor.location: Appendix C SUPERSEDED marker, Appendix D
minor.issue: Appendix C carries a SUPERSEDED marker on rev-3 B1's row pointing to Appendix D. This is a clean institutional-memory pattern: each revision's findings get their own appendix; superseded closures are marked forward, not deleted. A reader following the rev-3 B1 closure finds 'SUPERSEDED by rev-4 B1 rewrite (see Appendix D)' and can trace the full history without navigating five JSON files. The chain scales: rev-5, rev-6 would each get a new appendix with the same forward-marker pattern. No structural concern — this is noted as minor to explicitly confirm the pattern was evaluated rather than overlooked.

B1_property_test_rewrite.evidence: The rev-4 rewrite eliminates all three silent-pass surfaces identified in rev-3/rev-4 reviews. (1) No isinstance filter: the assertion path `assert redacted_extracted != raw_extracted` and `assert redacted_value != raw_value` are reached for every T without a type gate before them. The only branch before these asserts is `if raw_extracted is None: continue` (scalar path) and `if raw_value is None: continue` (container path), which are the specified allowed-only skip. (2) value_provider is None skip eliminated: the discovery loop now contains `assert node.value_provider is not None` — this is a mandatory assertion, not a conditional skip. Any walker regression that produces a None value_provider for a Sensitive-marked node at with_values=True causes a loud test failure with a precise diagnostic message citing the model name and path. (3) Defensive except eliminated: the `dict(raw_extracted)` and iteration at container paths are not wrapped in any try/except. A walker contract violation (e.g., value_provider returning a non-iterable for a [*] path) propagates as-is. (4) Empty sensitive_nodes guard: `assert sensitive_nodes` fails loudly if a type-driven manifest entry carries no Sensitive-marked paths, preventing vacuous-pass on a misclassified entry. (5) Implementer cross-check: a six-item mandatory checklist is added to the Task 19 body; the Done-When item for B1 requires inspection against the checklist before sign-off. The rev-4 type-agnostic path-aware != invariant structurally closes the known silent-pass family. See warning A1 for the latent nested-Sensitive-in-Sensitive concern, which is future-scope, not a remaining gap in the rev-4 invariant itself.

M1_unconditional_removal.evidence: Tasks 4, 13, and 14 all state 'REMOVE unconditionally' with a rationale citing CLAUDE.md No Legacy Code Policy. Task 4 Step 4 reads: 'REMOVE _TOOL_REQUIRED_PATHS["set_source"] (and any companion _TOOL_OPTIONAL_PATHS["set_source"]) unconditionally in the same commit that promotes the handler.' The conditional 'if redundant, remove' language from rev-3/rev-4 is gone. Task 13 per-tool bullets say 'REMOVE _TOOL_REQUIRED_PATHS[tool] ... unconditionally in the same commit.' Task 14 repeats the same unconditional form. Done-When item (~line 2050) adds an explicit grep-clean criterion for the _TOOL_REQUIRED_PATHS sweep. The audit error_class change from 'MissingRequiredPaths' to the ValidationError-class string is documented as a known one-way door in the one_way_doors section below; the project's No Legacy Code Policy and absence of external audit consumers constitute the mitigation.

M2_grep_step.evidence: Task 4 Step 4 adds a dedicated 'MissingRequiredPaths test-pin update' subsection with: (a) the exact grep command (`grep -Rn '"MissingRequiredPaths"\|MissingRequiredPaths' tests/ src/elspeth/web/ --include='*.py'`); (b) per-match decision rules (update if the scenario invokes the promoted tool, leave if the scenario invokes a non-promoted tool); (c) explicit identification of test_service.py:367 as a set_pipeline pin assigned to Task 14 (not Task 4) with a note explaining why it must not be updated prematurely; (d) the specific updated assertion form (`assert 'ValidationError' in result.message`). Tasks 13 and 14 cite 'see Task 4 Step 4 canonical procedure' and add per-tool reminders. Done-When adds a grep-clean criterion. The hidden-ordering coupling (Tasks 13/14 cannot be executed without reading Task 4) is an intentional shared-contract pattern rather than an architectural defect: the procedure is documented once with clear forward-references, which is cleaner than duplicating 15 lines of guidance three times.

A2_freeze_elision_comment.evidence: Task 1's TraversalNode definition in the plan body (lines ~435-463) now includes a multi-paragraph docstring explicitly documenting the freeze-elision rationale: '(1) _SensitiveMarker instances are constructed once (at Annotated[...] definition time, module load) and never mutated; (2) TraversalNode is produced inside walk_model_schema and discarded after iteration — there is no long-lived reference path that would expose mutation of a marker; (3) All other metadata entries are either pydantic.FieldInfo (built-in immutable for our usage) or scalar/None.' The docstring also adds a forward-looking guard: 'If a future change introduces stateful metadata objects, ADD a freeze_fields() call here AND a deep_freeze() of metadata — do not rely on the type signature alone.' This satisfies the rev-3 A2 resolution requirement ('document design assumption inline') and the rev-4 W1 carry-forward.

blast_radius.files_touched: ~16 source + 2 config (redaction.py, redaction_telemetry.py, tools.py ~8 promoted handlers, service.py minor, 1 GH Actions workflow, 1 CI script) plus ~14-15 test files
blast_radius.weighted_score: redaction.py = 2x, tools.py = 2x, service.py = 2x. Test files ~14 x 0.5 = 7. Config/CI = 2 x 0.5 = 1. Total weighted approx 20-23.
blast_radius.recommendation: Proceed as one phase. The tasks are interdependent through manifest population: Task 19's property test requires Task 16 to have completed, and the adequacy guard is structurally red between Tasks 9 and 16. Splitting into Phase 2A/2B would create a coordination boundary where a mandatory CI gate is chronically red, which violates the project's gate discipline. The 20-task structure with tracer-bullet at Task 4 and bulk manifest population deferred to Task 16 is the correct management strategy for this blast radius. The task sequencing manages risk adequately: foundation first (Tasks 1-3), tracer bullet validates integration assumptions (Task 4), adequacy guard built before the population wave (Tasks 9-12), population wave then green-lights the guard (Tasks 13-16).

manifest_replaces_TOOL_REQUIRED_PATHS.risk: Medium — audit error_class string change from 'MissingRequiredPaths' to 'ValidationError' for promoted tools
manifest_replaces_TOOL_REQUIRED_PATHS.mitigation_present: Yes — mitigated by project policy (CLAUDE.md No Legacy Code Policy) and confirmed absence of external audit consumers. The MissingRequiredPaths test-pin sweep (Task 4 Step 4 + grep-clean Done-When criterion) ensures no test is left asserting the stale string for a promoted tool. The audit table is internal; no external consumer parses error_class strings. Acceptable as a deliberate break.

policy_hash_snapshot_committed.mitigation_present: Yes — bootstrap_redaction_snapshot.py regenerates idempotently; pre-merge regeneration step in Task 16-final-b. Direction-aware label gate detects weakening.

label_gate_workflow.mitigation_present: Yes — direction-aware; can be relaxed without re-review of prior decisions.

pydantic_model_validate_at_dispatch.risk: Medium — ValidationError disposition change from HTTP 500 (catch-all) to ARG_ERROR
pydantic_model_validate_at_dispatch.mitigation_present: Yes — ToolArgumentError wrap explicit in every promoted handler; per-tool regression tests assert error chain (pydantic.ValidationError as __cause__); ARG_ERROR routing confirmed at service.py:2480.

notes: Rev-4 B1 is genuinely and structurally closed. The type-agnostic path-aware != invariant is a substantive improvement over both the rev-2 string-in-serialized and rev-3 isinstance(str)-gated designs. All three silent-pass families identified across four review passes are addressed: (1) isinstance gate eliminated; (2) value_provider is None skip converted to mandatory assert; (3) defensive except absent. The rev-4 closure is not partial — it closes the family of silent-pass modes that have converged three reviewers at this file. The one new latent concern (warning A1: nested Sensitive inside a Sensitive container) is future-scope; no current manifest entry triggers it. M1 and M2 are cleanly closed: unconditional removal with rationale, grep command, per-match decision rules, and explicit Done-When grep-clean criteria. A2 is closed: TraversalNode docstring now carries the freeze-elision rationale and a forward-looking guard. The three remaining warnings (A1 nested-Sensitive composition, A2 Hypothesis strategy resolution risk, A3 Done-When grouping) are genuine improvement opportunities but none represent the class of silent-pass architectural failure that has driven prior CHANGES_REQUESTED verdicts. The plan is implementable as written.
